About Me

Building at the Edge of Electricity

I'm Nitish Kumar, a Sydney-based Electrical Engineer holding a B.Tech in Electrical & Electronics Engineering from Maharaja Agrasen Institute of Technology (GGSIPU, Delhi) — graduated First Division with a CGPA of 8.71 — and currently completing a Master of Engineering at Western Sydney University, where I achieved a High Distinction (91) in Engineering Project 1.

Currently working as a CNC Technician at Gerry's Glass, where I manage and maintain three CNC machines, perform advanced fault diagnosis, and produce technical electrical drawings. I bridge the gap between mechanical precision and electrical intelligence.

Outside the workshop, I design and build ambitious personal projects — from orbital tracking systems to AI-powered command centers — driven by a passion for engineering that refuses to sit still.

Featured Work

Engineering
Projects

PROJECT — 001

2-Axis
Satellite Tracker

A precision dual-axis tracking system capable of following low-earth-orbit satellites in real time. Combines a high-torque linear actuator with a 380kg.cm servo for azimuth and elevation control, achieving sub-degree pointing accuracy for antenna alignment.

Linear Actuator Servo Control Embedded C RF Systems Arduino UNO
Primary Actuator Linear Actuator
Servo Torque 380 kg.cm
Axes Azimuth + Elevation
Controller Arduino UNO + Joystick
How It Works
01Dual-axis mechanical frame — A cardboard-and-servo prototype mast provides the elevation axis via a small hobby servo at the tip, while the azimuth (pan) axis is driven by a high-torque rotary base.
02Arduino UNO control core — An Arduino UNO reads analog joystick input and maps X/Y deflection to PWM signals sent to both servo channels simultaneously.
03Dish pointing — The blue parabolic dish mounted at the apex follows commanded angles, simulating real-time antenna steering toward a moving satellite pass.
04Power — A 9V alkaline battery powers the Arduino, with servo current drawn directly from the regulated 5V rail.

PROJECT — 002

High-Power
Inverter

A custom-designed square-wave inverter delivering 15V at 10A continuous output. Built from discrete components with full H-bridge topology and gate driver circuitry, designed for driving inductive loads and motor control applications.

Power Electronics H-Bridge Gate Drivers MOSFETs PWM Control
Output Voltage 15V DC
Output Current 10A Continuous
Waveform Square Wave
Topology Full H-Bridge
How It Works
01H-Bridge switching — Four MOSFETs are arranged in an H-bridge. Diagonal pairs switch ON alternately at the desired frequency, reversing current direction through the load to produce a square wave.
02Gate driver circuit — Dedicated gate driver ICs provide the high-current gate pulses needed to switch power MOSFETs cleanly, preventing shoot-through and reducing switching losses.
0315V / 10A output — The DC bus is held at 15V. At 10A continuous, the design handles 150W, with MOSFET selection and heatsinking matched to the thermal dissipation requirements.
04PWM frequency control — Output frequency is set by a 555-timer or microcontroller PWM signal, making the inverter adaptable for motor drive or AC simulation applications.
Build Gallery High-Power Inverter — 15V / 10A Square Wave
INVERTER Top view — dual hand-wound toroidal inductors + PP capacitor bank
INVERTER Angled view — MOSFET H-bridge on aluminium heatsink with active cooling fan
Dual toroidal inductors (hand-wound)
Red polypropylene capacitor bank
MOSFET H-bridge on aluminium heatsink
Active cooling fan + perfboard construction
PROJECT — 003

Ionic
Thruster

An electrohydrodynamic propulsion device that generates thrust through ionized air, requiring no moving parts. Powered by a custom high-voltage supply with a Cockcroft-Walton voltage multiplier to achieve the kilovolt-range fields needed for ion acceleration.

HV Power Supply Cockcroft-Walton Voltage Multiplier EHD Propulsion High Voltage
HV Stage Cockcroft-Walton Multiplier
Propulsion Electrohydrodynamic
Moving Parts Zero
Medium Ionized Air
How It Works
01Cockcroft-Walton multiplier — A ladder network of diodes and capacitors steps up a low-frequency AC input through cascaded half-wave rectifier stages, multiplying the peak voltage with each stage.
02Kilovolt-range field generation — The multiplied HV output is applied across a sharp emitter electrode and a collector. The intense electric field in the gap ionizes surrounding air molecules.
03Electrohydrodynamic thrust — Ionized air molecules accelerate from emitter to collector under the electric field, dragging neutral air molecules with them via collisions — producing a thrust with zero moving parts.
04Silent propulsion — With no mechanical components, the thruster operates completely silently and is nearly maintenance-free, making it ideal for research into silent UAV propulsion.
Build Gallery Ionic Thruster — HV Supply + Cockcroft-Walton Multiplier
IONIC HV driver board — flyback transformer + polypropylene capacitor bank
IONIC Power supply unit — live dual voltmeter reading 11.8V / 0.00A
IONIC Cockcroft-Walton multiplier — capacitor ladder stage (front)
IONIC Cockcroft-Walton multiplier — HV rectifier diode stack (rear)
IONIC Thruster emitter array — twin cylindrical spiked ion-emission electrodes

PROJECT — 004

Solar HHO
Generator

A solar-powered water electrolysis system using potassium hydroxide (KOH) electrolyte to produce oxyhydrogen gas (HHO) for clean energy experimentation. Optimized electrode geometry maximizes gas production per watt of solar input.

Solar Power Electrolysis KOH Electrolyte Clean Energy Electrochemistry
Power Source Solar PV
Electrolyte KOH (Potassium Hydroxide)
Output Gas HHO (Oxyhydrogen)
Application Renewable Energy Research
How It Works
01Solar panel input — A photovoltaic panel delivers DC current directly to the electrolysis cell, eliminating grid dependency and making the system fully off-grid and renewable.
02KOH electrolyte — Potassium hydroxide dissolved in water dramatically increases conductivity, reducing the cell resistance and boosting gas production efficiency compared to plain water electrolysis.
03Water electrolysis — Current flows between stainless steel electrode plates, splitting H₂O into hydrogen gas at the cathode and oxygen at the anode. The mixed output is oxyhydrogen (HHO).
04HHO collection — Gas is bubbled through a water trap/bubbler before collection, removing electrolyte mist and producing clean oxyhydrogen for combustion or research testing.
Build Gallery Solar HHO Generator — KOH Electrolysis Cell
HHO Electrolysis cell core — stainless steel electrode plate stack on threaded rod assembly
HHO Full system — electrolysis cell + KOH solution + bubbler water trap + gas output tubing
Stainless steel electrode plates (hand-cut, stacked)
KOH electrolyte solution in sealed clear housing
Bubbler / water trap for clean HHO output
Pneumatic push-fit gas plumbing
PROJECT — 005

Tesla
Coil

A resonant air-core transformer system generating high-frequency, high-voltage electrical discharges through electromagnetic resonance. Built with a custom primary and secondary winding geometry, capacitor bank, and spark gap topology for maximum streamer length.

Resonant Circuits High Voltage RF Engineering Spark Gap Magnetics
Topology Spark Gap SSTC
Output High-Voltage HF Arc
Coupling Air-Core Resonant
Capacitor Bank Custom MMC Design
How It Works
01Primary LC tank — A mains-powered transformer charges a multi-mini-capacitor (MMC) bank. When voltage exceeds the spark gap breakdown threshold, the gap fires and dumps energy into the primary coil.
02Resonant energy transfer — The primary coil and secondary coil are tuned to the same resonant frequency. Energy transfers efficiently via magnetic coupling, with the secondary voltage rising dramatically due to the turns ratio.
03Toroid terminal — A toroidal top terminal distributes the HV field evenly, reducing corona losses and directing the energy into long streamers that discharge into the surrounding air.
04RF discharge — Streamers emanating from the toroid are radio-frequency arcs at hundreds of kilohertz — far too fast to cause electrocution, though still carrying sufficient energy to be hazardous at close range.

PROJECT — 006

Jarvis
AI Command Center

A personal AI-powered home automation hub inspired by Tony Stark's JARVIS. Built on a Mac Mini running Python for natural language processing, with an ESP32 microcontroller acting as the hardware bridge for real-time IoT device control and sensor integration.

Python ESP32 Mac Mini NLP / AI IoT / Automation MQTT
Compute Mac Mini (Local AI)
MCU Bridge ESP32
Language Python 3
Interface Voice + Programmatic
How It Works
01Wake word + speech recognition — A Python script running on the Mac Mini continuously listens for a wake word, then captures voice commands and processes them through a local or API-based NLP pipeline.
02Intent parsing — Natural language commands are parsed into structured intents (e.g., "turn on the lights" → GPIO HIGH on pin 5), matched against a library of 14+ registered automations.
03ESP32 hardware bridge — The Mac Mini sends serial or Wi-Fi (MQTT) messages to an ESP32, which acts as the physical I/O controller for relays, LEDs, sensors, and other connected devices.
04Feedback loop — Jarvis confirms actions via synthesised speech output, closing the human-machine interaction loop and providing status updates for complex multi-step automations.
